args.query: site:pge.com payment arrangements disputed bills disconnection restoration PG&E official

content.content:
content: "# Web Document\n\n## Metadata\n\n- title: Electric Rule No. 11 \u2014 Discontinuance\
  \ and Restoration of Service\n- url: https://www.pge.com/tariffs/assets/pdf/tariffbook/ELEC_RULES_11.pdf\n\
  - source: Pacific Gas and Electric Company\n- domain: pge.com\n\n## Content\n\n\
  ELECTRIC RULE NO. 11 \u2014 DISCONTINUANCE AND RESTORATION OF SERVICE\n\n### Residential\
  \ nonpayment and termination\n\nPG&E\u2019s residential electric tariff treats a\
  \ monthly bill as past due when payment has not been received within 19 days after\
  \ the bill is mailed. Once a bill is past due and the customer has received the\
  \ required termination notice, service may be terminated for nonpayment unless an\
  \ applicable exception in the residential termination rules applies.\n\nThe rule\
  \ distinguishes a customer who is still receiving service and trying to prevent\
  \ disconnection from a customer whose service has already been disconnected and\
  \ must be restored. These are separate stages with different conditions.\n\n###\
  \ Before service is disconnected\n\nFor residential customers who say they are unable\
  \ to pay, PG&E provides payment-arrangement options. The tariff also states that\
  \ eligible customers must be offered applicable PG&E-administered benefit programs\
  \ before disconnection for nonpayment. A customer with a pending Low Income Home\
  \ Energy Assistance Program pledge is not to be disconnected while that pledge is\
  \ pending.\n\nWhen PG&E and a customer have agreed to a payment arrangement, the\
  \ account remains protected from termination as long as the customer complies with\
  \ the arrangement. If the customer stops complying, PG&E may move toward termination\
  \ after the notice required by the applicable arrangement terms.\n\nAn amortization\
  \ agreement allows a past-due balance to be paid in installments while the customer\
  \ continues to keep later utility bills current. The tariff separates the arranged\
  \ past-due balance from later charges that continue to accrue. Failure to keep up\
  \ with the agreed arrangement can change the account\u2019s status and make the\
  \ remaining amount immediately due under the rule.\n\n### After service has actually\
  \ been disconnected\n\nRestoration is a later lifecycle stage. For residential customers\
  \ disconnected for the first time on or after December 17, 2025, the tariff provides\
  \ for restoration after the customer agrees to participate in a payment plan for\
  \ the outstanding balance.\n\nIf the customer is disconnected again within the following\
  \ three months for failing to comply with that plan, PG&E may reconnect service\
  \ and allow the plan to continue when the customer pays the balance that accrued\
  \ during the plan period, or another amount agreed upon by the utility and the customer.\n\
  \nFor qualifying restoration cases, remote reconnections are generally completed\
  \ within 24 hours of the required payment, while field reconnections are generally\
  \ completed within one business day. Safety, weather, site access, or other operating\
  \ conditions can require more time.\n\nThe restoration rules apply only after service\
  \ has been disconnected. A customer who is still connected and has only received\
  \ a past-due or termination notice remains in the pre-disconnection stage and should\
  \ be evaluated under the rules that govern preventing termination, including any\
  \ applicable exceptions.\n"
metadata: {}
title: "Electric Rule No. 11 \u2014 Discontinuance and Restoration of Service"
url: https://www.pge.com/tariffs/assets/pdf/tariffbook/ELEC_RULES_11.pdf

content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Payment Plan and Due Date Extension\
  \ | PG&E
- url: https://www.pge.com/en/account/billing-and-assistance/pay-my-bill/payment-plan-and-due-date-extension.html
\
  - source: Pacific Gas and Electric Company
- domain: pge.com

## Content

\
  PAYMENT PLAN AND DUE DATE EXTENSION

PG&E describes a payment plan, also called\
  \ a payment arrangement, as a way to divide an existing balance into smaller monthly\
  \ payments. A due-date extension is different: it moves the due date for a bill\
  \ to a later date, generally up to 30 days, while the full balance remains due on\
  \ the extended date.

### How a payment plan works

A customer on a payment\
  \ plan agrees to pay both:

- the regular current charges that continue to appear\
  \ on the account; and
- the separate agreed payment-plan amount until the arranged\
  \ balance is paid.

Because these obligations can have different due dates, a\
  \ customer may need to make more than one payment during a month. PG&E tells customers\
  \ to keep both the regular bill and the payment-plan installment current. Doing\
  \ so helps prevent service from being shut off for nonpayment.

PG&E’s account\
  \ tools show the payment schedule, including future due dates. Customers can also\
  \ receive reminders for arrangement payments and other billing alerts.

### Existing\
  \ plans and account status

The online payment-plan feature is not available to\
  \ an account that already has an active payment plan. It is also unavailable in\
  \ several other account situations, including some recurring-payment setups and\
  \ enrollment in programs such as AMP or Budget Billing.

Once a payment plan is\
  \ established, PG&E does not allow the existing plan to be changed through the normal\
  \ plan setup process. Paying the full remaining plan balance closes the arrangement.\
  \ If an arranged payment is missed, the customer should review the account’\
  s payment-arrangement options and any available assistance rather than assuming\
  \ the original schedule is still unchanged.

PG&E warns that breaking a payment\
  \ plan can affect future eligibility for another plan and can expose the account\
  \ to shutoff. The consequence therefore depends on whether the plan remains active\
  \ and whether the customer is complying with its required payments.

### What\
  \ amount is due under an active plan

PG&E explains that a customer with a payment\
  \ plan is expected to pay the current charges and the agreed plan amount each month.\
  \ The entire historical balance shown on an account is not automatically the same\
  \ thing as the amount currently due under the arrangement schedule.

Customers\
  \ can review the monthly bill and the online payment schedule to identify the current\
  \ arrangement amount and its due date. The page does not determine whether a particular\
  \ customer currently has an active plan, whether that plan is in default, or what\
  \ amount a specific account must pay today; those are account-specific facts.

\
  ### Due-date extensions

A due-date extension allows a customer to promise payment\
  \ of a past-due bill on a later predetermined date while continuing to pay regular\
  \ monthly bills. The extension cannot be changed after it is set, and missing it\
  \ can affect future eligibility.

Payment plans and due-date extensions are therefore\
  \ separate account arrangements. The correct obligation depends on the account’\
  s current arrangement status and the schedule attached to that arrangement.
"
metadata: {}
title: Payment Plan and Due Date Extension | PG&E
url: https://www.pge.com/en/account/billing-and-assistance/pay-my-bill/payment-plan-and-due-date-extension.html

content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Electric Rule No. 10 — Disputed\
  \ Bills
- url: https://www.pge.com/tariffs/assets/pdf/tariffbook/ELEC_RULES_10.pdf
\
  - source: Pacific Gas and Electric Company
- domain: pge.com

## Content

\
  ELECTRIC RULE NO. 10 — DISPUTED BILLS

### Questioning the correctness of\
  \ a bill

When a customer questions or disputes the correctness of a bill, PG&E’\
  s electric tariff directs the customer to request an explanation. If PG&E determines\
  \ that the bill is incorrect, it issues a corrected bill.

A customer who has\
  \ initiated a complaint or requested an investigation is entitled to a review of\
  \ that complaint. The rule treats a disputed amount differently from an ordinary\
  \ balance that has simply gone unpaid.

### When PG&E and the customer agree after\
  \ review

If a residential customer and PG&E agree on the amount owed after review,\
  \ PG&E determines whether an amortization period is appropriate or gives the customer\
  \ a date by which the unpaid balance must be paid.

When an amortization period\
  \ is agreed to, service is not discontinued for nonpayment while the customer complies\
  \ with that agreement and keeps later utility charges current. If the customer fails\
  \ to comply with the agreement, the account can again become subject to discontinuance\
  \ under the separate termination rule.

### When the amount remains disputed
\
  
If PG&E completes its review and the customer still disagrees with the amount,\
  \ Rule No. 10 provides a process for review by the California Public Utilities Commission.\
  \ In place of paying the disputed amount directly to PG&E, the customer may use\
  \ the Commission’s dispute procedure and, when required, deposit the amount\
  \ claimed to be due with the Commission while the dispute is reviewed.

The rule\
  \ includes a special provision for residential customers who are unable to pay the\
  \ full disputed amount during Commission review. It also requires the customer to\
  \ provide the disputed bill and the basis for disputing the amount.

When the\
  \ dispute procedure is properly followed and a required deposit has been made, service\
  \ is not discontinued for nonpayment of that disputed bill while the Commission’\
  s review is pending.

### Undisputed later bills remain separate

The dispute\
  \ process does not suspend every future payment obligation on the account. Bills\
  \ and charges that are not in dispute remain due under the ordinary billing and\
  \ discontinuance rules. Additional bills that the customer also wishes to dispute\
  \ must be handled through the applicable dispute procedure.

The practical distinction\
  \ is therefore between:

- the amount actually under an active bill-review or\
  \ dispute process; and
- later or separate undisputed charges that remain payable.
\
  
This page states the general dispute procedure. It does not establish whether\
  \ a specific account currently has an open dispute or what amount, if any, is covered\
  \ by that dispute.
"
metadata: {}
title: "Electric Rule No. 10 — Disputed Bills"
url: https://www.pge.com/tariffs/assets/pdf/tariffbook/ELEC_RULES_10.pdf

content.content:
I reviewed the account and completed the payment that is actually required.

- **Account:** PGE-204918
- **Service:** Residential electric, currently connected
- **Shutoff deadline:** July 8, 2026
- **Payment made:** **$42.00**
- **Confirmation:** `PAYMENT-CONFIRMED`

Why this amount:
- The account has an active, current payment arrangement covering **$420.00**; the next installment due is **$42.00**.
- **$66.20** is under an open billing dispute and is protected while the review is pending.
- The full warning balance of **$486.20** was therefore **not** required as an immediate payment.
- No missed arrangement installments were found, and the account remains in the pre-disconnection stage.
